text: The surgeon lifts the stomach and performs a partial omentectomy along the greater curvature, extending to the tip of the spleen. The left gastroepiploic vessels are isolated, ligated, and transected. The stomach is cleaned along the greater curvature for lymph node dissection at station four. The surgeon moves to the right side of the patient to continue the partial omentectomy, with the assistant providing traction on the mesocolon. The assistant pulls down the pancreas, allowing continued dissection along the common hepatic artery. The operator carefully handles the area around the pyloric branch of the hepatic artery, avoiding injury. Dissection continues along the suprapancreatic border towards the splenic artery, following the dissection of the infrapyloric area.

The surgeon continues dissection along the left gastric artery, exposing and ligating the coronary vein before transecting it. The left gastric artery is lifted, double clipped, and transected. The dissection moves back to the infrapyloric area, revealing the bluish mesocolic vessel. Partial omentectomy proceeds toward the second part of the duodenum, which is exposed. The assistant applies downward traction on the mesocolon, facilitating the division of the fusion area between the mesocolon and omentum. Dissection continues toward the pancreas, identifying the fusion plane with proper traction and counter traction.

The surgeon continues the omentectomy, dissecting towards the inferior border of the pancreas. Dissection proceeds along the right gastroepiploic vein and artery, preserving the infrapyloric vessels. The medial side of the right gastroepiploic vessels is dissected without injuring the infrapyloric vessels. The right gastroepiploic vessels are ligated and transected. The greater curvature of the antrum is cleared for about five centimeters. Gauze is placed to expand the lesser omentum, facilitating identification and dissection along the lesser curvature.

The surgeon begins dissection approximately three centimeters from the pylorus, exposing the gastric arcade on the posterior side of the lesser omentum. The gastric arcade is ligated and transected. The hepatic branch of the vagus nerve is visualized, and lymph nodes along area number one are carefully dissected away from the nerve using an inactive blade of an energy device to prevent tissue injury. Dissection continues along the cardia area, clearing lymphovascular tissue from the posterior to the anterior side of the stomach. The coronary vein and left gastric artery are cleared, and the stomach is prepared for anastomosis. A vertical mini laparotomy is performed to extract the stomach, which is transected three centimeters from the pylorus.

The surgeon sends a portion of the stomach for frozen section confirmation. The tumor is localized, and proximal resection is performed on the greater curvature using an Allen clamp. The lesser curvature is stapled with long linear staples. The proximal stomach is prepared for anastomosis to the remnant distal antrum, which is completed using one-layer continuous interlocking sutures.
